channel.title: Qiskits on dev-undergrad
channel.description: Recent content in Qiskits on dev-undergrad

item.title: Ch.6 The CHSH Game
item.description: $$ \sim * \backsim $$ Table of Contents § 15. Nonlocal Games § 16. The CHSH Game Exercises Solutions $$ \backsim * \sim $$ § 15. Nonlocal Games The CHSH game falls within the class of nonlocal games. The game is a cooperative game where Alice and Bob work together to achieve an outcome. There also exists a referee who ensures Alice and Bob abide by a strict set of guidelines.

item.title: Ch.5 Superdense Coding
item.description: $$ \sim * \backsim $$ Table of Contents § 13. Superdense Coding Protocol Analysis § 14. Qiskit Implementation Exercises Solutions $$ \backsim * \sim $$ In the last chapter, we studied the quantum teleportation protocol, which transmitted one qubit using two classical bits of communication, at the cost of one entangled e-bit. The superdense coding protocol achieves the exact inverse. It allows for the transmission of two classical bits using on qubit of quantum information, at the cost of one e-bit.

item.title: Ch.4 Quantum Teleportation
item.description: $$ \sim * \backsim $$ Table of Contents § 9. Quantum Entanglement § 10. Entanglement as a resource § 11. Teleportation No-Cloning Theorem Protocol Analysis § 12. Teleportation using Qiskit Testing Teleportation Exercises Solutions $$ \backsim * \sim $$ By this point, you should have a good understanding of Qiskit as a language. We know how to define any n-qubit state and build a circuit to operate on it. The chapters will now start to contain more information outside of purely code cells.

item.title: Ch.3 Quantum Circuits
item.description: $$ \sim * \backsim $$ Table of Contents § 7. Building Quantum Circuits Classical Bits Sampler Primitive § 8. Quantum Circuits Review Exercises Solutions $$ \backsim * \sim $$ So far, we’ve discussed working with single-qubit and multi-qubit states. We’ve also covered various operators and used the .evolve() method to apply these operators to statevectors. Now it’s time to start building our own Quantum Circuits. These circuits allow us to visualize the change of information and trace the evolution of qubits through a series of quantum gates.

item.title: Ch.2 Multiple-Qubit States
item.description: $$ \sim * \backsim $$ Table of Contents § 4. Multi-Qubit Quantum Gates SWAP Gate Controlled Operations CX / CNOT § 5. Tensor Products § 6. Partial Measurements Exercises Solutions $$ \backsim * \sim $$ § 4. Multi-Qubit Quantum Gates Now that we understand single-qubit gates, let’s explore multi-qubit operators, which act on more than one qubit / statevector. A multi-qubit state $$|XY\rangle$$ is the tensor product of two single-qubit states:

item.title: Ch.1 Single-Qubit States
item.description: Preliminary Info This tutorial series assumes you are already knowledgeable in basic quantum mechanics (state vectors/wave functions), statistics, linear algebra, and Python. This series is for those who want to focus on directly learning IBM Qiskit, the Python library. To learn Qiskit along with the necessary math, visit IBM Quantum Learning. Once the necessary Qiskit concepts are introduced, we will delve directly into quantum computing theory, where chapters will become more math-intensive.
